title: 📝 Mock Exam A — Full paper (exam-style)
blurb: A complete timed-style mock exam in the professor's format: mostly concept short-answers (explain / why / contrast), one Keras CNN code question, and a CNN calculation. Covers the whole syllabus with emphasis on NN fundamentals + CNN.

knowledge_points.title: Part 1 — NN & CNN core
knowledge_points.recap: Core exam topics: NN fundamentals (loss/activation/optimization) plus CNN concepts, a CNN calculation, and a Keras code question.

questions.q: Neural-network fundamentals. (a) For a binary classification task, name the appropriate **output activation** and **loss function**, and explain why they pair well. (b) For 10-class classification, what changes? (c) Briefly, why is a plain **linear (identity) output + MSE** a poor choice for classification?
questions.answer: (a) Use **sigmoid** output + **binary cross-entropy**. The sigmoid squashes the logit to a probability in $[0,1]$; binary cross-entropy is the negative log-likelihood of a Bernoulli, so its gradient w.r.t. the logit is simply $(\hat{y}-y)$ — clean, well-scaled gradients and no vanishing when the prediction is confidently wrong. (b) Use a **softmax** output over 10 units + **categorical cross-entropy** (softmax generalises sigmoid to a probability distribution over classes). (c) Linear output + MSE does not produce probabilities, penalises correct-but-confident outputs oddly, and gives small/vanishing gradients in the saturated region, so it trains slowly and calibrates poorly for classification.
questions.extend: The 'clean gradient' cancellation is why cross-entropy is preferred over MSE with sigmoid/softmax.

questions.q: Which statement best explains why a **convolutional layer** is preferred over a **fully-connected layer** for images?
questions.answer: A convolutional layer uses **local receptive fields** and **weight sharing**, giving far fewer parameters and **translation-equivariant** feature detection, whereas a fully-connected layer connects every input pixel to every neuron and ignores spatial structure.

options.text: It uses weight sharing and local receptive fields, so it has far fewer parameters and detects features regardless of position.
It always has more parameters, which increases accuracy on images.
It removes the need for any activation function.
It guarantees the network cannot overfit.

questions.q: CNN calculation. An input feature map is $32\times32\times3$. A convolutional layer applies **16 filters** of kernel size $5\times5$, **stride 1**, and **'same' (zero) padding** of 2. (a) Give the output spatial size (H and W). (b) Give the number of output channels. (c) Compute the total number of **trainable parameters** in this conv layer (weights + one bias per filter). Recall output size $= \lfloor (W - K + 2P)/S \rfloor + 1$.
questions.answer: (a) $H = W = \lfloor(32-5+2\cdot2)/1\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 31/1 \rfloor + 1 = 32$, so the output is $32\times32$ (padding='same' preserves spatial size at stride 1). (b) **16** output channels (one per filter). (c) Weights per filter $= 5\times5\times3 = 75$; plus 1 bias $= 76$ per filter; total $= 76 \times 16 = \mathbf{1216}$ trainable parameters.

questions.q: Keras code. You are given `x_train, y_train, x_test, y_test` for a 10-class image dataset with inputs of shape $28\times28\times1$ and **integer** labels. Write Keras code that (a) builds a small CNN classifier (at least one Conv2D + pooling, then a Dense head), (b) compiles it with an appropriate optimizer and loss, (c) fits it for a few epochs, and (d) evaluates test accuracy.
questions.answer:
A minimal, correct answer:

```python
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import layers, models

model = models.Sequential([
    layers.Input(shape=(28, 28, 1)),
    layers.Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding='same'),
    layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2)),
    layers.Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding='same'),
    layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2)),
    layers.Flatten(),
    layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'),   # 10 classes
])

# integer labels -> sparse categorical cross-entropy
model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy',
              metrics=['accuracy'])

model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=5, batch_size=64, validation_split=0.1)

test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
print('Test accuracy:', test_acc)
```

Key marks: **softmax** 10-unit output, **sparse_categorical_crossentropy** (labels are integers, not one-hot), and calling `.evaluate` on the test set.
questions.extend: If labels were one-hot, switch the loss to `categorical_crossentropy`.

questions.q: Classical ML — **k-Nearest Neighbours**. (a) Is KNN a parametric or non-parametric method, and where does its 'training' cost go? (b) Contrast the effect of a very **small k** vs a very **large k** on the decision boundary (bias/variance). (c) Why does feature **scaling** matter for KNN?
questions.answer: (a) KNN is **non-parametric** and a **lazy learner**: there is essentially no training — it just stores the data; the cost is at **inference**, when it searches for the nearest neighbours. (b) **Small k** (e.g. k=1) gives a very flexible, jagged boundary → **low bias, high variance** (sensitive to noise/overfitting); **large k** smooths the boundary → **high bias, low variance** (can underfit). (c) KNN relies on a **distance metric** (e.g. Euclidean); features on larger numeric scales dominate the distance, so features must be **normalised/standardised** for a fair comparison.
questions.extend: Odd k avoids ties in binary voting.

knowledge_points.title: Part 2 — Breadth (advanced topics)
knowledge_points.recap: One question per advanced topic: RNN/embeddings, attention/Transformer, reinforcement learning, generative AI/LLM, and GNN/PINN or EDA/Agentic.

questions.q: Sequence models. (a) Explain why plain **RNNs** suffer from the **vanishing gradient** problem on long sequences. (b) Name one **LSTM gate** and describe how the LSTM architecture mitigates this problem.
questions.answer: (a) Backpropagation through time repeatedly multiplies by the recurrent weight/derivative at each step; when these factors are $<1$ the gradient shrinks **exponentially** with sequence length, so early time steps receive almost no learning signal (long-range dependencies are lost). (Factors $>1$ cause the exploding-gradient variant.) (b) The **forget gate** (also input and output gates) controls a **cell state** that is updated mostly by **addition**, creating a near-constant-error 'conveyor belt'. Gradients can flow along the cell state without repeated multiplicative shrinkage, so the LSTM preserves long-range information much better.
questions.extend: GRUs achieve similar gating with fewer parameters (update + reset gates).

questions.q: In **self-attention**, what is the role of the **query, key, and value** vectors?
questions.answer: Each token's **query** is compared (dot product) with every token's **key** to produce attention weights (via softmax), and those weights are used to take a weighted sum of the **value** vectors. This lets every token gather context from all other tokens in parallel.

options.text: The query-key dot products give attention weights (after softmax) that weight the value vectors summed into each output.
Queries and keys are the network's learned biases; values are the labels.
They are three independent CNN filters applied to the image.
The value vector alone determines the attention weights; keys are unused.

questions.extend: Scaling by $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the softmax gradients well-behaved.

questions.q: Reinforcement learning — discounted return. An agent receives the reward sequence $r_1=2,\ r_2=0,\ r_3=-1,\ r_4=5$ and then the episode ends. Using discount factor $\gamma = 0.5$, compute the **discounted return from time step 1**, $G_1 = r_1 + \gamma r_2 + \gamma^2 r_3 + \gamma^3 r_4$. Also state in one sentence what $\gamma$ controls.
questions.answer: $G_1 = 2 + 0.5(0) + 0.25(-1) + 0.125(5) = 2 + 0 - 0.25 + 0.625 = \mathbf{2.375}$. The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0,1]$ controls how much **future** rewards are valued relative to immediate ones: small $\gamma$ is myopic (favours near-term reward), $\gamma$ near 1 is far-sighted.

questions.q: Generative AI. (a) What problem with a plain **LLM** does **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** address? (b) Outline the two main steps of a RAG pipeline. (c) Name one benefit of RAG over simply fine-tuning the model on the documents.
questions.answer: (a) A plain LLM answers only from **frozen parametric knowledge** learned at training time, so it can be out-of-date and tends to **hallucinate** facts it does not know. (b) **Retrieve** — embed the query, search a vector store / knowledge base for the most relevant document chunks; **Generate** — feed those chunks into the prompt as context so the LLM answers grounded in them. (c) RAG can be **updated instantly** by changing the document store (no retraining), provides **source citations/traceability**, and is cheaper than repeatedly fine-tuning.
questions.extend: Retrieval quality (embeddings + chunking) is usually the bottleneck for RAG accuracy.

questions.q: GNNs for EDA. (a) In a **message-passing GNN**, describe in one or two sentences how a node updates its representation. (b) Why are GNNs a natural fit for **EDA** problems such as netlist/circuit analysis? (c) What does it mean for a GNN to be **inductive**, and why is that useful for EDA?
questions.answer: (a) Each node **aggregates messages** from its neighbours (e.g. sum/mean/max of transformed neighbour features), then **combines** that aggregate with its own features through a learnable update (and a nonlinearity); stacking layers widens the receptive field over the graph. (b) A **netlist is literally a graph** — cells/gates are nodes and wires/nets are edges — so a GNN respects the circuit's connectivity and permutation-invariance instead of forcing it into a grid or sequence. (c) **Inductive** means the model generalises to **unseen graphs/nodes** (it learns functions of local structure, not fixed node IDs), so a GNN trained on some designs can predict on **new circuits** — essential for reusable EDA surrogate models (e.g. congestion / timing / power prediction).
questions.extend: GraphSAGE is a classic inductive GNN; earlier transductive methods could not handle new nodes.
